mcqs.question: Which IEEE standard defines Ethernet?
mcqs.explanation: IEEE 802.3 is the standard for Ethernet. 802.11 is for Wi-Fi, 802.1Q for VLANs, and 802.15 for Bluetooth.

mcqs.question: What is the size of a MAC address?
mcqs.explanation: MAC addresses are 48 bits (6 bytes), typically represented as 12 hexadecimal digits.

mcqs.question: In Ethernet frame structure, what comes immediately after the Preamble?
mcqs.explanation: The 7-byte Preamble is followed by the 1-byte Start Frame Delimiter (SFD) which is 10101011 in binary.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum Ethernet frame size (including header and trailer)?
mcqs.explanation: Minimum frame size is 64 bytes: Destination MAC (6) + Source MAC (6) + Type/Length (2) + Data (46) + FCS (4) = 64 bytes.

mcqs.question: What protocol does Ethernet use to detect collisions?
mcqs.explanation: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD) is used in half-duplex Ethernet to detect collisions on shared media.

mcqs.question: Which type of Ethernet cable is commonly used for connecting computers to switches?
mcqs.explanation: Straight-through cables are used to connect unlike devices (computer to switch, router to switch). Crossover cables connect like devices.

mcqs.question: What is the maximum data payload size in a standard Ethernet frame?
mcqs.explanation: The maximum payload (data) size is 1500 bytes, as defined by the Type/Length field value. Total frame = 1518 bytes.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the FCS field in an Ethernet frame?
mcqs.explanation: Frame Check Sequence (FCS) is a 4-byte CRC checksum used to detect data corruption during transmission.

mcqs.question: Which Ethernet standard supports speeds up to 1 Gbps?
mcqs.explanation: 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) supports 1 Gbps over twisted pair cables. 10BASE-T is 10 Mbps, 100BASE-TX is 100 Mbps, 10GBASE-T is 10 Gbps.

mcqs.question: What is the maximum cable length for 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX?
mcqs.explanation: Both 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX have a maximum cable length of 100 meters (328 feet) from the host to the hub/switch.

mcqs.question: In a switched Ethernet network, which layer does the switch operate at?
mcqs.explanation: Ethernet switches operate at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2), using MAC addresses to forward frames.

mcqs.question: What is the broadcast MAC address?
mcqs.explanation: The broadcast address is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (all 48 bits set to 1), used to send frames to all devices on the network.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the Ethernet frame header?
mcqs.explanation: Ethernet frame header contains: Preamble (7B), SFD (1B), Destination MAC (6B), Source MAC (6B), Type/Length (2B). Sequence number is not part of standard Ethernet.

mcqs.question: What happens when a collision is detected in Ethernet?
mcqs.back: The transmitting device stops sending, sends a jam signal, waits for a random backoff time, and attempts to retransmit

options.text: The transmitting device stops and waits for random backoff time before retrying
The switch automatically isolates the port
The frame is resent automatically without delay

mcqs.question: Which Ethernet standard uses fiber optic cabling for long distances?
mcqs.explanation: 100BASE-FX is Fast Ethernet over fiber optic cable, supporting longer distances (up to 2 km for half-duplex, 10 km for full-duplex).

mcqs.question: What is auto-negotiation in Ethernet?

options.text: Automatic speed and duplex setting between devices

mcqs.explanation: Auto-negotiation allows two connected Ethernet devices to automatically determine the highest speed and duplex mode they both support.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of VLAN (802.1Q) tag in Ethernet frames?
mcqs.back: To identify which VLAN a frame belongs to, enabling network segmentation and broadcast domain separation

options.text: To identify VLAN membership and segment networks

mcqs.question: What type of addressing does Ethernet use at Layer 2?
mcqs.explanation: Ethernet operates at Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) and uses MAC (Media Access Control) addresses for frame delivery.

mcqs.question: In full-duplex Ethernet, why is CSMA/CD not required?

options.text: Because simultaneous transmission and reception eliminates collision possibility

mcqs.explanation: Full-duplex allows simultaneous sending and receiving on separate wire pairs, so collisions cannot occur and CSMA/CD is not needed.

mcqs.question: What is a jumbo frame in Ethernet?
mcqs.back: An Ethernet frame larger than the standard 1518 bytes, typically up to 9000 bytes, used to reduce overhead in high-speed networks

options.text: A frame used for testing network speed
A frame larger than standard maximum size (typically 9000 bytes)
